topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Arthur had spent forty years perfecting the art of miniature model shipbuilding. His workshop, usually a sanctuary of quiet precision, now felt like a high-alert zone. Little Leo, at seven, had discovered Arthur’s dusty collection of tiny galleons and declared them the pinnacle of existence. Now, every afternoon, Leo would commandeer the workbench, his small fingers meticulously gluing minuscule rigging onto a ship that was barely larger than a thumbnail. Arthur watched, a knot tightening in his chest, as Leo, with an intense, almost feverish concentration, adjusted a sail with the delicate tweezers. When Leo finally held up his creation—a slightly lopsided frigate with painstakingly applied, albeit slightly uneven, canvas—and beamed, Arthur felt a flush creep up his neck. "Look, Dad! It’s the fastest ship ever!" Leo’s enthusiasm was infectious, but the sheer scale of his devotion to this hobby, which Arthur had once cherished as a quiet pastime, now felt overwhelming, a spotlight shining directly on his lifelong, slightly nerdy passion.

[story 2]

"Just a little more varnish, sweetie," Arthur murmured, trying to keep his voice level as Leo leaned over the workbench, his breath fogging the glass of a tiny, meticulously detailed pirate ship. Leo didn't hear the instruction; his eyes were locked on the microscopic details of the hull. He was applying a layer of clear coat with the focus of a surgeon. Arthur felt a distinct tightening around his jaw. He’d always loved the meditative quality of sanding wood, the slow, deliberate process of bringing a raw piece to life. Now, that focused intensity was being channeled entirely into recreating the exact shade of barnacle gray on a model. "It needs to be perfect, Dad," Leo insisted, his tone serious and unwavering. The way Leo scrutinized the tiny imperfections, his brow furrowed in deep concentration, made Arthur want to melt into the floorboards. He cleared his throat, feeling suddenly clumsy and exposed under the gaze of his son’s singular, consuming interest.

[story 3]

The scent of aged wood and thin glue usually brought Arthur a sense of calm. Today, it brought a churning anxiety. His grandson, Finn, was perched on a stool, completely absorbed in assembling a complex, multi-masted sailing vessel. Finn wasn't just playing; he was operating with a level of dedication that bordered on obsession. Arthur had spent decades mastering the delicate art of scale modeling, treating each piece with reverence. Seeing Finn meticulously aligning a tiny mast, his small hands steady and sure, made Arthur’s stomach clench. He wanted to retreat, to hide behind the towering shelves of completed ships. "That rigging looks incredible, Finn," Arthur managed, his voice coming out a little too high. Finn looked up, his eyes bright with triumph, and he held up the finished piece, shaking slightly with pride. The sheer, unadulterated focus radiating off the boy made Arthur feel profoundly scrutinized, as if his entire history of quiet tinkering had been laid bare for judgment.
